item.title: Brooks — Liner Notes for the Revolution
Frith — Performing Rites
item.description: Author: Simon Frith Title: Performing Rites: On the Value of Popular Music Year: 1996 Publisher: Harvard University Press Type: Book Frith’s central argument is that value judgments in popular music — what counts as “good,” “authentic,” “serious,” or “real” — describe social processes rather than in...

item.title: George — The Death of Rhythm and Blues
item.description: Author: Nelson George Title: The Death of Rhythm and Blues Year: 1988 Type: Book (Pantheon Books) A history of Black popular music from rhythm and blues through the 1980s that doubles as an argument about institutional destruction.

item.title: Gilroy — The Black Atlantic
item.description: Author: Paul Gilroy Title: The Black Atlantic: Modernity and Double Consciousness Year: 1993 Type: Academic book (Harvard University Press) A theoretical reframing of Black cultural production that argues against nationalist frameworks — “American music,” “British music,” “African music” — in favor ...

item.title: Hebdige — Subculture
item.description: Author: Dick Hebdige Title: Subculture: The Meaning of Style Year: 1979 Type: Academic book (Methuen) A study of postwar British subcultures — teddy boys, mods, skinheads, punks — that argues style is a form of meaning-making, and that British youth subcultures used Black music as raw material for c...

item.title: Keightley — Reconsidering Rock
item.description: Author: Keir Keightley Title: “Reconsidering Rock” Year: 2001 Publisher: Cambridge University Press (in The Cambridge Companion to Pop and Rock, ed.

item.title: Wilder — American Popular Song
item.description: Author: Alec Wilder Title: American Popular Song: The Great Innovators, 1900–1950 Year: 1972 Publisher: Oxford University Press Type: Book (edited and with an introduction by James T.

item.title: Stevie Wonder
item.description: Genres: Soul, Pop soul, funk, art pop Scene: Detroit / Motown Records Era: 1961–present (primary period: 1971–1976) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The riff that opens “Superstition” — that dirty, stacked Hohner Clavinet line — Stevie Wonder built alone, laying down the drums first and then overdubbing ...

item.title: The Beach Boys
item.description: Genres: Surf rock, vocal surf, baroque pop, sunshine pop, psychedelic pop, progressive pop, pop rock Scene: Hawthorne / Hollywood, California Era: 1961–present (primary period: 1963–1970) RYM · Apple Music · Spotify The Beach Boys are one instrument made of five throats.
